video_title: The Problem With AI Nobody Wants to Admit ft. Cysic
gist: Leo Fan, founder of Cysic, argues that 'crypto + AI' narratives without real product delivery are ephemeral; Cysic differentiates by building GPU software that creates genuine demand on its decentralized compute network, layering ZK verification and agentic coding tools to pursue a 'verifiable economy' vision spanning crypto and traditional data centers.

insights.claim: Current AI agents consume large amounts of tokens but frequently fail to deliver real outcomes, making the agentic economy narrative largely unproven and potentially short-lived without genuine product creation.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Investors should discount pure 'agentic economy' plays that lack measurable task completion rates; the space is producing cost without value, which historically precedes a narrative collapse.
insights.quote: the agent they really can cost you a lot of tokens but without delivering the real job there which is like somehow disappointing

insights.claim: Crypto + AI narratives without real product delivery will disappear within months, making them structurally similar to other empty crypto narrative cycles.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: High-conviction signal from an infrastructure builder with direct visibility into AI agent performance; supports a short thesis on pure narrative-driven crypto-AI tokens.
insights.quote: if we just say like AI crypto without uh say like doing some real development there then then it's basically nothing. It's just some fake narrative that will disappear in the public like in several months

themes.theme: ZK Proof Market Maturation and Declining Demand

insights.claim: ZK proof generation demand has materially declined over the past several months as the initial wave of ZK L2 projects (Near ecosystem in particular) wound down, forcing Cysic to pivot to AI inference workloads.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Concrete demand-side evidence that ZK proving markets are consolidating; decentralized prover networks dependent solely on ZK are facing revenue pressure, validating a pivot toward AI workloads.

insights.claim: ZK proof generation efficiency has improved 10x in roughly 6 months: proving one Ethereum block dropped from requiring ~1,490 H100-class GPUs to just 16 GPUs.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Rapid efficiency gains are compressing hardware revenue per proof, threatening the economics of dedicated ZK prover networks; software moat matters more than hardware scale.
insights.quote: previously by previously I mean like about 6 months ago if you want to proof one Ethereum block, you need 160 uh 1490 GPU cards to to to do so. But right now, you only need 16 GPU cards

insights.claim: ZK is now more mature and accessible: ZKVMs allow developers to write circuits in Rust or Python rather than domain-specific languages like Cairo, substantially lowering the barrier to entry.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Lower ZK circuit complexity unlocks a broader developer base, which could accelerate demand for verifiable compute even as per-proof costs fall; net impact on proving market volumes is positive long-term.

insights.claim: The 2021-2022 ZK hype cycle, despite being overblown, genuinely accelerated years of ZK development, and the ecosystem is now in a stronger technical position than during the hype peak.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Hype cycles in crypto can have positive externalities on underlying technology development even when the token narratives fade; ZK is a case study for evaluating the residual value of 'hype-funded R&D'.

themes.theme: Decentralized Compute Network Design and Differentiation

insights.claim: Most decentralized GPU compute platforms fail because they are passive marketplaces waiting for external demand, subsidized purely by token emissions; the differentiating move is to own the software stack that generates proprietary demand.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Platform-vs-product distinction is the key underwriting question for decentralized compute networks; networks that also build high-performance software applications on top create self-sustaining demand loops.
insights.quote: we do it like very very differently. So in addition to creating the this like platform we actually we have some real product

insights.claim: Cysic's GPU software reduced proving time for one L2 block from 1.5 hours to 18 minutes, making it the largest single prover (>50% share) for multiple near-protocol ZK rollups at peak.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Software performance advantage, not hardware headcount, drove >50% market share concentration — software moat is durable and defensible even as raw GPU supply commoditizes.
insights.quote: proving like one block takes about like more than one hour to prove like one block but with our GPU software we can reduce the time from like 1.5 hours to just uh 18 18 minutes

insights.claim: Cysic has a community GPU pool of tens of thousands of GPUs, making it one of the largest decentralized GPU networks in crypto — critical for meeting peak proving demand.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Hardware network effects compound the software moat; this combination positions Cysic as a credible provider for institutional-scale ZK and AI inference workloads.

insights.claim: Cysic's four-layer architecture — blockchain coordination layer (Cosmos SDK), hardware layer (GPU/mobile/laptop), ZK verification layer (ZKVM), and product layer — is a complete vertical stack rather than a point solution.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Vertical integration across all four layers creates switching costs and cross-layer margin capture, but also raises execution risk compared to focused point solutions.

insights.claim: ZK proof verification (as opposed to generation) is cheap enough to run on a smartphone in under one second, enabling mobile devices to participate in decentralized proof markets as verifiers.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Mobile-verifier participation dramatically expands the addressable pool of network nodes and could drive consumer-facing DePIN narratives beyond GPU farms.
insights.quote: to verify a zip proof is quite easy. You can do it on your phone uh in less than like one second

themes.theme: AI Inference Verification and the 'Verifiable Economy'

insights.claim: The long-term vision for verifiable AI compute is that users can cryptographically confirm which model actually ran their inference request — solving the 'model bait-and-switch' problem where data centers could silently substitute cheaper models.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: As AI spending scales, enterprise buyers will demand model-level auditability; the first network to offer cryptographic proof of model execution at production latency/cost will capture significant B2B premium.
insights.quote: people may become like uh not trusting that since like you can say like the quality of the of the output of the model will somehow degrade and you will become suspicious that probably like it's not running upwards 4.8 is running some some version of the sonnet

insights.claim: ZK proofs are currently too slow for AI inference verification at scale; Cysic is using data analytics and sampling techniques as a near-term bridge while targeting a custom ZK ASIC as the long-term solution.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: There is no production-ready ZK solution for AI inference today; the market window for sampling-based verification is temporary, and the winner will be whoever solves ZK performance for AI workloads first.
insights.quote: ZK is still very slow for this like AI inference result. Yeah since AI inference is like way much complicated than proving just one Ethereum block

insights.claim: A ZK ASIC optimized for integer operations (rather than floating-point matrix multiplication like GPU) would deliver dramatically better performance-per-watt for ZK proof generation, making it viable for AI inference verification.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Custom ZK silicon is the plausible endgame for verifiable AI compute; the company that tapes out a production ZK ASIC at the right time could capture hardware moat similar to early Bitcoin ASIC manufacturers.
insights.quote: GPU is optimized for the floating floating point matrix multiplication. It's but for ZK is like doing this like notch integer notch integer operation. So it's like not optimized for ZK

insights.claim: Cysic's internal agentic coding workflow (now productized as 'Scops') generated over 500,000 lines of code internally and produced code that runs 10-15% faster than human-written equivalents.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Performance-superior AI-generated code is a credible differentiator from commodity AI coding tools; if the 10-15% performance claim holds externally, Scops has a strong hook for compute-sensitive workloads.
insights.quote: the performance of this code is like I I would say at least 10 to 15% like faster than the than the codes written by by human

insights.claim: The CLI version of Scops (called 'Humaniz') reached over 2,000 downloads per day organically, validating consumer demand before a formal web product launch.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Organic CLI traction at 2,000+ downloads/day before marketing spend indicates genuine product-market fit; the web version launch could accelerate Cysic's transition from crypto-native to general developer audience.
insights.quote: right now as you can see humaniz has like more than 2,000 downloads per day

insights.claim: Scops was conceived ~4-5 months before the market recognized the importance of agentic coding loops, giving Cysic a timing advantage over current Cursor/Codex incumbents in the reinforcement-loop coding segment.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Early mover positioning in agentic coding loops with integrated compute verification could make Scops defensible; however, Cursor/Codex have much larger distribution networks.
insights.quote: when we designed like the SCOPS like about four or five months ago there is like no not much thing about uh the rough loop or the the coding loop

insights.claim: Cysic deliberately avoided early ZK ASIC tape-out despite investor pressure because ZK algorithm fragmentation made it too risky to commit to a single algorithm at $4-5M cost with no ability to patch post-tape-out failures.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Peer projects that rushed into ZK ASIC tape-outs at the peak of hype have since disappeared; Cysic's capital discipline is a differentiating survival trait and a framework for evaluating other early-stage hardware crypto projects.
insights.quote: chip tip out really cost us something. It cost us probably like four or five million to do the chip tip out. Plus you need to spend huge amount of like effort to make sure like all the design there all the implementation they are correct since you just got one shot

insights.claim: The strategy of building superior GPU software first to capture market share and validate demand — rather than immediately pursuing ASIC hardware — allowed Cysic to remain solvent while peers who rushed to tape out ASICs exited the market.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: In nascent hardware markets with algorithm uncertainty, software-first strategies outperform hardware bets; this framework applies broadly to DePIN and AI accelerator startups.

insights.claim: Cysic bootstrapped its hardware network by building the highest-performance mining pool for third-party projects (e.g., ADOM), allowing contributors to earn real tokens from those projects while simultaneously accumulating Cysic points pre-TGE — a dual-incentive flywheel.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Dual-incentive bootstrapping (real yield from partner projects + speculative airdrop) is a more durable supply-side acquisition strategy than pure token emission; lowers the platform's own token dilution burden during cold-start.

insights.claim: Post-TGE, Cysic's CS token is trading at 2-3x TGE price in a difficult market, and the project is generating real external revenue — both cited as metrics of success beyond pure token price.
insights.speaker: Leo Fan
insights.so_what: Positive returns post-TGE in a bear market combined with external revenue signals genuine product-market traction; however, self-reported revenue claims warrant independent verification.
insights.quote: I I believe at in the current current market market condition like we have already like at least two or 3x compared with with a TG price
